danger_signals.visual_form: Large, distinctly designed enemy sprites; arena-like spaces

environmental_storytelling.example: Alsedo (Fairy village) attacked; visual deterioration signals invasion scope
Fairy Queen Eleanora travels to Purapril seeking aid; narrative justifies hero encounter
Companions return to home regions when Shion departs; environmental loyalty markers
Distinct village aesthetics (Fairy/Dwarf/Dragon/Darkworld); faction/region identity through design
Escalating swords, armor, boots; visual upgrade chains signal growing capability against invaders
Distinct regional bosses; visual identity ties to local faction/threat

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Randomly attacks enemies (cosmetic); conjures healing hearts when Shion low on health
Breaks open walls; uncovers hidden rooms; randomly digs up coin fountains
Flies alongside; randomly attacks enemies with throwing scythe
Temporary removal from action during boss fights; signals elevated threat
Patrol regions; attack on sight; indicate territorial control
Seek hero aid; offer quests; provide supplies; express fear/hope based on invasion status

notes: Wonder Boy in Monster World communicates world-state primarily through narrative exposition and NPC interaction, supplemented by environmental design. The game uses regional companions as faction-loyalty indicators and environmental storytellers. Danger is conveyed through enemy encounter density and boss design rather than explicit visual warning systems. The Princess's imprisonment represents political catastrophe, while the Fairy Queen assumes provisional leadership, creating a power vacuum narrative. Two versions exist with distinct thematic framing: Genesis/Master System uses medieval fantasy invasion; Turbografx (The Dynastic Hero) reframes as technological conquest. Equipment progression serves dual narrative/mechanical function: visual demonstration of growing hero capability against escalating threats.
